death.cause: Likely mercury poisoning from husband's tinsmithing trade

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her mother Candace Sage Gilbert, who died in 1805.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-three from her husband's trade
narrative_blocks.body: Candace died at age forty-three in 1835 — one year after her husband Solomon Churchill Jr., a tinsmith. Family record attributes both deaths to the mercury used in tin manufacturing. Together they had traveled town to town selling their wares, with their five children left orphaned in 1835.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

death.cause: Likely mercury poisoning from husband's tinsmithing trade

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her mother Candace Sage Gilbert, who died in 1805.

narrative_blocks.body: Candace died at age forty-three in 1835 — one year after her husband Solomon Churchill Jr., a tinsmith. Family record attributes both deaths to the mercury used in tin manufacturing. Together they had traveled town to town selling their wares, with their five children left orphaned in 1835.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

death.cause: Likely mercury poisoning from tinsmithing trade

career.title: Tinsmith / itinerant tinware seller
career.notes: Tinsmith by trade — taught by Enoch Kelsey. He and wife Candace went from town to town selling their wares. Both died young (he 42, she 43); deaths attributed to mercury used in the tin manufacturing process.

narrative_blocks.header: Mercury tinsmithing killed husband and wife
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon was a tinsmith by trade, taught by Enoch Kelsey of Berlin. He and Candace traveled town to town selling their tinware. Both died young — Solomon at forty-two in 1834 and Candace at forty-three the following year — deaths the family attributed to the mercury used in the tin-making process.

bio.photo_notes: Joint photo with wife Hermina M. Adhemar Elton (X02736). Compiler's research collection.

sources.short: Find a Grave — West Lane Cemetery

naming_inspiration.description: Given name 'Gilbert' carries the maternal grandmother's family surname forward — Hooker Gilbert was his maternal grandfather.

career.title: Wagon manufacturer
career.organization: Churchill & Sweeney (partnership with W. L. Sweeney)
career.notes: Formed partnership with W. L. Sweeney in 1860; ran successful wagon manufacturing business in Rock Island IL for 24 years until dissolved due to Cyrus's declining health. Died of consumption at home.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut wagon-maker built Rock Island partnership
narrative_blocks.body: Cyrus left Connecticut for Illinois and in 1860 formed a partnership with W. L. Sweeney that ran a successful wagon manufacturing business in Rock Island for twenty-four years.

narrative_blocks.header: Watched Rock Island grow from log cabins
narrative_blocks.body: At age four her family settled in Rock Island Illinois — then called Stephenson — making the trip from Pennsylvania by wagon and water. She grew up watching the trading village of a few log cabins become a city of nearly forty thousand people. She died at age 86 at her daughter LuLu's home in Blue Island Illinois.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her paternal aunt Laura Churchill Colvin Fickas, her father Cyrus's eldest sister.
Middle name 'Giles' likely honors maternal uncle Gilbert Churchill or echoes the Gilbert family name from his great-grandmother Candace Gilbert's line.

death.cause: Surgical complication at St. Anthony's Hospital

career.title: Manager
career.organization: H. J. Churchill Commission Co. (Rock Island House Block)
career.notes: Manager of H. J. Churchill Commission Co.

career.title: Advertising staff
career.notes: Rejoined the Argus in 1925; member of advertising staff at death.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Herbert Churchills span eighty Rock Island years
narrative_blocks.body: Herbert Jenkins Churchill named his son Herbert Jenkins Jr. in 1911, who in turn named his own son Herbert J. Churchill III in 1931 — three consecutive generations of Herberts rooted in Rock Island Illinois. The first Herbert managed a commission company and worked Argus advertising until his 1934 death.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Rock Island Memorial Park
The Rock Island Argus (newspaper, multiple dates)
Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Maternal uncle whose daughter Sarah he married in 1807.

naming_inspiration.description: Given the surname 'Hooker' as his first name in honor of his mother Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert's family. First-name use of the maternal surname is unusual and emphatic — he later married into the Hooker line as well, his second wife Sarah Hooker (H00432) being his maternal first cousin.

military_service.unit: Multiple — Capt. Gad Stanley's Co (1776), Capt. Lankton's Co under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regt (1777), Capt. Matthew Cole's Co (1779)
military_service.notes: Three documented enlistments: (1) June 1776 — four months in Capt. Gad Stanley's Company at the Battle of Harlem Heights; (2) summer 1777 — three months in Capt. Lankton's Company under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment; (3) July 1779 — ten days in Capt. Matthew Cole's Company at the Battle of New Haven (Tryon's Raid). Placed on the federal pension list in 1838. DAR Ancestor #A045110. Source: Berlin-Peck Memorial Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Revolutionary War enlistments and 1838 pension
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert enlisted three times during the Revolution: four months in June 1776 in Capt. Gad Stanley's Company at the Battle of Harlem Heights; three months in summer 1777 in Capt. Lankton's Company under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment; and ten days in July 1779 in Capt. Matthew Cole's Company at the Battle of New Haven during Tryon's Raid. He was placed on the federal pension list in 1838 and is recorded as DAR Ancestor #A045110.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Sarah Hooker in 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert's first wife Candace Sage died May 15, 1805. Two years later on June 30, 1807 he married Sarah Hooker — his maternal first cousin and the daughter of his uncle Samuel Hooker Jr. The marriage united two grandchildren of Samuel Hooker via his daughter Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert and his son Samuel Hooker Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Both spouses died within two days December 1840
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker died December 4, 1840 at Kensington Connecticut at age 77. Hooker Gilbert followed her two days later on December 6, 1840 at age 86 — together they had been married for thirty-three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Two grandchildren of Samuel Hooker reunited 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert and Sarah Hooker were the marriage point where two branches of the Hooker line came back together. His mother Elizabeth and her father Samuel Jr. had been siblings — both children of Samuel Hooker of Kensington. The cousin marriage produced no documented children of its own but closed a fifty-year gap between the two branches.

parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Allyn, dau. of Capt. Thomas Allyn of Windsor and Abigail (dau. of Rev. John Warham).
parents.father_research_notes: Ebenezer Gilbert, merchant of Hartford (d.1726). Will inventoried £4,455 including 4 enslaved persons; served on 1717-1718 school committee. Not added as separate entry.

narrative_blocks.body: Moses Gilbert was baptized in Hartford in 1707 and died in Berlin, Connecticut in 1803 at approximately 96. His son Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776. His grandson Sylvanus, son of Ebenezer, enlisted in the same army and also died in service.

narrative_blocks.header: His father's 1726 will inventoried four enslaved people
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Gilbert's father Ebenezer Gilbert was a Hartford merchant who died in 1726. His estate of £4,455 included four enslaved people itemized by price: a negro (£100), a negro woman (£45), a boy (£100), a child (£30). He had also served on a 1717-1718 committee that divided Kensington into five school districts — using Gilbert's River as a boundary line.

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter Sarah married my son Hooker Gilbert in 1807.

narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hooker married Moses Gilbert in February 1734 and bore him six children in Berlin, Connecticut. Her son Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776, at age 34. Ebenezer's own son Sylvanus later died in the same army.

cross_connections.display_label: Niece by brother Samuel Jr. who married her son Hooker.
Her daughter Sarah married my son Hooker Gilbert in 1807.

burialCemetery.notes: Major Gilbert family burial ground in Kensington CT — the village where Hooker Gilbert (H00420, 1751-1840) lived on the ancient Gilbert farm at 551 Christian Lane (built 1709 by Ebenezer Gilbert, sold to City of New Britain 1906). Resting place of Hooker Gilbert and both his wives, four of his Gilbert children with Candace Sage, and other extended Gilbert kin. Adjacent to the family's ancestral landmark.
